ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for red bean soup with tangerine peel. FOODON_03311737 (adzuki bean food product) is the closest parent class covering adzuki/red bean-based preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chinese red bean dessert soup. A00LC (Legume-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for red bean soup with tangerine peel as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of red bean soup with tangerine peel is approximately 250 g, served warm as a dessert or snack in Chinese cuisine.

nutrient_source.title: Adzuki Beans, Mature Seeds, Cooked, Boiled, Without Salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for dried tangerine peel flavouring contribution and rock sugar dilution adjustment for soup preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked adzuki beans (FDC ID 173735) to reflect a soup preparation with approximately 60% bean solids and 40% water/liquid, plus minor rock sugar addition (~5 g per 100 g soup). Energy and macros adjusted proportionally. Tangerine peel contributes negligible macronutrients at typical usage levels. Sodium is low as no salt is added in traditional preparation. This is the closest defensible approved-source match; no Singapore HPB Foodo entry was found for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Adzuki beans, boiled (GI = 29)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 29 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled adzuki beans (Vigna angularis), which is the primary ingredient of this soup. The soup preparation (dilution with water and minor rock sugar addition) may slightly raise effective GI compared to plain boiled beans, but the bean matrix dominates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (29 × 16.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = approximately 11.8 g. This is classified as a low-GI food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~8 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (~280 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Red bean soup with tangerine peel is very low in sodium and provides meaningful potassium, making it a suitable dessert choice for individuals managing hypertension, provided no salt is added during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 29) driven by high dietary fibre and resistant starch in adzuki beans
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 29 and approximately 3.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, this soup produces a blunted postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include a standard bowl (250 g) as an occasional dessert while monitoring total carbohydrate intake (~40 g per serving).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high soluble fibre from adzuki beans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The negligible saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and soluble fibre content of adzuki beans support LDL cholesterol management, making this dessert soup a heart-friendly option when consumed without added saturated fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Bean Soup with Tangerine Peel (陈皮红豆汤, Chen Pi Hong Dou Tang) is a traditional Chinese sweet dessert soup (tong sui) made by slow-simmering adzuki beans (red beans) with dried aged tangerine peel (chen pi) and rock sugar until the beans are tender and the broth is fragrant. The dish is a staple of Cantonese dessert culture and is widely served in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Guangdong province. The use of aged Xinhui tangerine peel — sometimes matured for 10 years or more — imparts a distinctive mellow citrus aroma and is believed in traditional Chinese medicine to aid digestion and regulate qi. Nutritionally, the soup is a moderate source of plant protein (~4.8 g per 100 g), high in dietary fibre (~3.8 g per 100 g), and very low in fat (~0.2 g per 100 g). Its low glycemic index of approximately 29 makes it one of the more diabetes-friendly dessert options in Chinese cuisine. The sodium content is naturally very low when prepared without added salt. The soup is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, is gluten-free, and is halal-compliant. It is typically served warm as a dessert or afternoon snack. Clinically, the high fibre and low GI profile support glycaemic control, while the potassium content and negligible sodium are favourable for blood pressure management. The main allergen concern is citrus sensitivity due to the tangerine peel.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry was found for red bean soup with tangerine peel as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked adzuki beans (FDC ID 173735) with proportional adjustment for soup dilution and minor rock sugar addition. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled adzuki beans, which is the primary ingredient. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically sound but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of the finished soup.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy cooked adzuki bean data (FDC ID 173735) to a soup matrix (~60% bean solids, ~40% water, ~5 g rock sugar per 100 g soup). This is the best available approved-source approximation. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for cooked adzuki beans in the USDA SR Legacy source and is not a characteristic nutrient of this dish. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked adzuki beans; plant-based foods do not naturally contain vitamin D. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this preparation. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked adzuki beans. 6. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food containing no animal products; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods. 7. Cholesterol (cholesterol_mg) is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food; cholesterol is naturally zero. 8. Monounsaturated fat (monounsaturated_fat_g) is 0.0 and trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as these are confirmed negligible/trace in cooked adzuki beans per USDA data. 9. GI of 29 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled adzuki beans; the soup preparation may marginally affect GI but the bean matrix dominates. 10. The tangerine peel used is Xinhui chen pi (新会陈皮), a geographically indicated product from Xinhui district, Jiangmen, Guangdong. 11. Allergen: citrus sensitivity — tangerine peel is a citrus product. 12. No HPB Singapore Foodo entry was identified for this specific dish during processing.
